mnemonics.title: File Allocation Methods
mnemonics.content: CONTIGUOUS: C - CONSECUTIVE BLOCKS, FAST BUT FRAGMENTS; LINKED: L - CHAINS BLOCKS WITH POINTERS, NO FRAGMENT BUT SLOW; INDEXED: I - INDEX BLOCK POINTERS, DIRECT ACCESS BUT EXTRA BLOCK NEEDED. REMEMBER: CLI - CONTIGUOUS LINKED INDEXED

mnemonics.title: Inode Contents
mnemonics.content: INode stores: MODE (permissions), LINKS (hard links count), UID (owner), SIZE, ATIME (access), MTIME (modify), CTIME (change), BLOCKS (pointers to data). REMEMBER: MAMA SIB - MODE ATIME MTIME ACCESS TIME SIZE INODE BLOCKS

mnemonics.title: Free Space Management Methods
mnemonics.content: BIT VECTOR: BITMAP IS FAST; LINKED LIST: POINTER CHAINING SLOW; GROUPING: GROUP FREE ADDRESSES; COUNTING: CONTIGUOUS RUNS STORE. REMEMBER: B L G C - BITMAP LINKED GROUP COUNTING

acronyms.usage: Provides common interface for different file system types like ext4, NTFS, FAT allowing them to coexist in one OS
Legacy file system using a table where each entry points to the next block in a chain, stored at beginning of partition
UNIX file system structure storing file metadata and data block pointers, identified by unique inode number
Kernel data structure containing all information about a file including metadata and allocation pointers
Method of addressing disk blocks by their logical sequence number rather than physical CHS (cylinder-head-sector)
Framework allowing users to create file systems without kernel modifications, runs as a userspace process

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of numbered parking spots (disk blocks). Each car (file) parks in consecutive spots. Easy to find car location, but when cars leave, scattered empty spots cannot fit large vehicles (external fragmentation).

Think of a treasure hunt where each clue (disk block) points to the next location. Blocks can be anywhere, each knows where the next is. No wasted gaps but must follow the chain to reach destination (no random access).

Like a book table of contents (index block). Each page (data block) has a number, and the index lists all page numbers in order. Jump to any topic directly without flipping through all pages (direct access).
Imagine a light board with one bulb per parking spot. Green (1) = occupied, Red (0) = empty. Scan quickly to find empty spots. Larger disks need bigger boards but scanning is still fast.
Think of a filing cabinet drawer (directory) with folder tabs (file names). Each folder contains an index card (inode/FCB) with folder contents and location info.

analogies.analogy: Just as a library organizes books using a catalog system (directory), shelves (disk blocks), and indexes (inodes), an OS file system organizes data with directories, storage blocks, and metadata structures.
A buffer cache is like keeping frequently used reference books on your desk rather than walking to the bookshelf every time. Saves trips (disk I/O) but requires memory space.
The superblock is like the blueprint of a library - it shows how many shelves exist, where the catalog is, what sections exist, and overall layout - essential for the library to function.

analogies.concept: File Mounting to Adding New Branch Library
analogies.analogy: Mounting a file system is like opening a new branch library and connecting it to the main library system - patrons can now access its books through the same catalog interface.
External fragmentation in contiguous allocation is like a parking lot with scattered small empty spaces that can't fit a large car, even though total empty space is enough.
